lines.text: Okay — before the bell. One thing, and I promise it's a good one.
lines.action: leans against the front desk

lines.text: This semester you're doing a project called 'Where I'm From.' You tell the class who you are. Where home is. What made you. Pairs. Presentation day at the end of the unit.
Five minutes. In front of people. Cool. Cool, cool, cool.
And — I already know what you're thinking. No, you don't pick your partner. I picked. On purpose. Not at random.
lines.action: taps two knuckles on the desk

lines.text: Ten bucks he does the thing where he says it's not random and it's so, so random.
Jordan, you're with Priya. Maya, you've got Deshawn.
Wren — you're with Alex.
...Huh.
lines.action: glances over, face doing something complicated

lines.text: Good luck.
lines.action: mouths it at Wren with a grin that doesn't quite hold

lines.text: Cool.
lines.action: doesn't look up from their notebook

lines.text: New city, new locker combo, new tragic-backstory partner. I can work with this. ...What does 'doesn't really do groups' even mean, exactly.
Okay, question. If I described my partner as a human filing cabinet, would that be mean, or accurate?
lines.action: snaps fingers softly

lines.text: I mean — Deshawn does alphabetize his Pokémon cards. Out loud.
That's what I'm SAYING.
Anyway. You got Alex, right? Rivera-Chen.
Yeah. We haven't really — talked yet, I guess.
Oh, you will. They'll talk your ear off about, like, dragons or whatever, then go silent for a week. It's a whole cycle.
Yeah, no, it's — it's a whole thing.
Okay but actually — here's a thought.
Everyone knows Alex doesn't really do groups. Like, at all. It'd almost be doing them a favor if the pairings got, I don't know, revisited.
And you're new, right? Teachers love the new kid who 'raises concerns.' Looks mature. Considerate, even.
You mention it to Alvarez, it's not even a big deal. Just — 'hey, some pairings feel kind of random, is that intentional.' That's it.
...Yeah, honestly, wouldn't even be that weird.
lines.emotion: small laugh, staring at his tray

lines.text: It'd just be easier. For everyone.
lines.action: snaps fingers once, softer

lines.text: Right?
Right. Sure. Easier for everyone means easier for her. But it's one sentence. It's basically nothing.
Hey — quick thing? Some people were saying the pairings feel kind of random. Is that... intentional?
It is, actually. Very intentional.
Right, no, I figured — I just, some people feel like it's not really fair.
Here's the thing.
lines.action: leans against the front desk, patient

lines.text: Pairings are staying as they are. For a reason. Random doesn't get you anywhere new. On purpose does.
...Okay. Yeah. That actually makes sense.
You alright?
Yep! Totally. Just checking. For a friend.

lines.action: arrives early, catches the tail end, says nothing, holds the look a beat too long, walks past

lines.text: Great. Love this. Love being 'checking for a friend' guy.
If this is the part where you tell me it's fine, I've heard it. Doesn't help.
lines.action: arms crossed, not looking over

lines.text: No, I was gonna ask if you've done the 'Where I'm From' thing before. At a different school.
...No. Never had to.
Cool. Same, actually. So we're both flying blind. Very professional.
...That's not comforting.
Didn't say it'd be comforting. Said we're flying blind together. There's a difference.

lines.action: shoulders drop half an inch, taps four times on the locker instead of speaking

lines.text: Library. After school. I have an idea for the structure. It's dumb, probably.
Cool, cool — dumb ideas are my favorite kind. Least resistance.
...Okay.
Okay, so — structure. I think we do it chronological. Boring but it works. Unless you have a better idea.
Depends. Does 'better' mean 'more work'? Because I have a system for avoiding that.
...Everything's a system with you, huh.
It's called being efficient. I prefer the word efficient.
...Sure. Efficient.
So what's your actual thing. For the project. Not the —
lines.action: gestures vaguely, doodling in the margin

lines.text: — the version you'd give a teacher.
...What do you mean, 'version'?
You've got a whole bit, don't you. The four-schools thing. 'Pretend you know where you're going.' It's a good bit. It's very polished.
I'm asking what's under the bit.
Nobody's asked it like that. Usually people just laugh at the bit and we move on.
You don't have to. It's not a test.
I just figured if we're doing this project, might as well not lie in it.
Okay. So — real version. Every time we move, right before we leave, I do this thing.
I buy a sticker. Gas station, gift shop, wherever. And I put it on the inside of my suitcase lid.
And there's this pin box — my mom got it for me after move number two — and I put one pin in it. From wherever we're leaving.
So the suitcase is just — layers of places. And the box rattles when I carry it, which everyone thinks is weird, but it's the only sound that's stayed the same for six years.
That's the real one. Not the bit.
...Six years. How many places is that.
Five. Six, counting here. I stopped explaining it because it makes people sad in a way I don't know what to do with.
I'm not sad. I'm —
That's kind of incredible, actually. In a terrible way.
Yeah. That's — that's exactly the right way to say it, actually.
Okay. Don't make it weird. I made this. It's a world. I've been building it since fourth grade.
lines.action: reaches into their bag, hesitates

lines.text: It's not — it's not a big deal.
lines.emotion: sliding a notebook page across the table

lines.text: ...Alex. This is a whole map. There's a legend. You made a legend.
It's dumb. Forget it—
No! I'm not forgetting it. What's this river called, I need to know immediately.
...It's called the Kettering. Long story.
lines.emotion: real smile, no delay this time

lines.text: Fifteen minutes. That's all it took.
Oh, you know. Cities, boxes, the usual — very Wikipedia summary.
Anyway — map idea. I think it could look really cool, like a physical timeline.
...Right. Map idea. Sure.
We could do pins for each city, string between them, detective-board energy.
Yeah. That works.

lines.action: goes back to doodling, taps four times on the table, more guarded now

lines.text: ...You okay?
It's fine.
Let's just get the outline done. I have somewhere to be.
That went smooth. Efficient. So why does it feel like I locked a door I didn't mean to.
Okay wait, tell them your project topic. The map thing. It's SO —
lines.action: snaps fingers, already laughing

lines.text: — it's like a treasure map to nowhere, right? 'X marks the spot, population: one weird kid.'

lines.emotion: laughs half a beat late, then looks at his tray

lines.text: No offense, obviously. It's just — very Alex, you know? Draws imaginary countries, cries about rivers.
lines.action: glances around the table checking who's laughing

lines.emotion: goes still, jaw tight, stares at their tray

lines.text: Right? Tell me that's not exactly it.
...Relax, it's a joke. God, why's everyone so —
lines.action: catches herself

lines.text: —okay, fine. Whatever.
It's actually a really good project though. Alex built a whole world. Maps, a legend, all of it.
...A legend. Cute.
lines.action: twirls a braid, recalibrating

lines.text: Honestly, that does sound kind of cool, no for real—
...Didn't know you two were, like, a whole thing now.
We're partners. Turns out that involves talking to each other. Wild concept.
Fine! Fine. Table's still open, by the way. Door's not locked or whatever.

lines.action: glances at player — surprise, then something warmer, gone as fast as it came

lines.text: First time all year my stomach didn't drop after I said something out loud.
Yeah, no, it's — kind of a weird pick, I guess.
Right?? See, I'm not even being mean, I'm just saying what everyone's thinking.
lines.action: snaps fingers once, satisfied

lines.emotion: laughs along, relieved the tension broke, doesn't look at Alex

lines.text: I have somewhere to be.
lines.emotion: flat, to no one in particular
lines.action: stands, tray in hand, walks off

lines.text: See, dramatic. Told you.
lines.action: watching them go, shrugs

lines.text: That was easy. That's the problem. That was so easy.
Alright — Alex and Wren, you're up.
I'm from a house where nobody really talks at dinner. Where I'm from is quiet — the kind you fill in yourself.
So I started filling it. I've been building a world since fourth grade. I know that's — whatever, a lot. But it's the loudest thing about me, so. Here it is.
Wait, Alex made a whole map? Since fourth grade?
...Guess so.
This is the Kettering River. It floods every year in the story. I don't know why I made it do that. Guess I like things that come back even when they wreck everything first.
lines.action: holds up the page, hand slightly unsteady

lines.text: Thank you, Alex. That took something.
lines.action: taps two knuckles on the desk, moving it along gently

lines.text: Wren?
Okay. Here it is. The trapdoor.
So. Where I'm from.
Honestly? I'm from a lot of places. Five schools in six years. I have a system for the first day — pretend you already know where you're going. Works pretty well, actually.
But the real thing — the thing under the system — is I've never had six years anywhere. I've had six years of goodbye. Just, different addresses for it.
I make friends fast because I have to. I get funny fast because if I make the joke first, nobody else gets to make it about me.
I didn't figure that out till kind of recently. Like — this week, recently.
So. That's where I'm from. It's not a place. It's more like a move I know how to make really, really well.
Thank you, Wren. That was —
lines.action: catches himself over-explaining

lines.text: That was honest. That's the assignment, actually. Well done.

lines.emotion: catches her eye across the room, small real nod

lines.text: Still standing. Huh.
So! Where I'm from. Buckle up, it's a tour.
I'm basically a professional new kid. Five schools, six years, I could write a survival guide. Chapter one: never sit at the first open seat.
Fun fact, I've lived in three time zones. My suitcase has more frequent flyer miles than most adults.
Anyway — that's me! Professional mover, part-time comedian, full-time—
—very fun at parties.
lines.emotion: catches herself right before something real

lines.text: ...Thank you, Wren. Great stuff.
lines.emotion: eyes lingering a beat too long

lines.text: Landed exactly where I aimed. So why's it feel like I missed.
Okay, honestly? Vibes are weird today. Can we just — make it not weird? Everyone's fine, right? We're all fine.
...Are we, though?
I mean — nobody's, like, mad. It's fine. It's whatever.
lines.emotion: laughs a half-second before it lands

lines.text: Honestly, easiest thing is just — everyone forgets about it by Monday. No for real, that's usually how it goes.
That IS usually how it goes. That's the whole system, actually.

lines.action: twirls a braid, doesn't leave

lines.text: The thing at lunch yesterday. You asking me to go to Alvarez about the pairings. Then the joke about the project. That wasn't nothing.
...It was a joke. I say jokes. That's kind of my whole thing.
Yeah, I know. But it was also kind of a test. See if I'd pick you over him. And I get why you did it, I just—
I don't want to keep passing tests I didn't agree to take.
...You think I do that on purpose?
I think you don't have to mean it for it to be true.
It's not — I'm not trying to be some mean-girl thing. I just. If people stop laughing at my jokes, they stop looking at me. That's just — that's how it's always worked.
...Yeah. I get that more than you'd think.
God, this got weirdly serious.
A little. Sorry. I'm bad at knowing when to stop.
...No, it's fine. Don't apologize for that part.
Didn't fix it. But that's the first true sentence we've said to each other all year.
Yeah. Let's just — let it be normal again.
See?? Easy. Everyone's chill. Vibes: fully restored.
...Thanks, Wren. Knew you'd get it.
lines.emotion: smile that doesn't reach her eyes

lines.text: Yeah. No problem.

lines.action: turns and walks off, Jordan trailing after, already talking about something else

lines.text: Peace. Great. Feels a lot like the six months before every move, actually — everyone smiling, nobody saying the thing.
Maya's table. Alex, alone at the edge, notebook out. Jordan, hovering in the middle like he's hoping nobody makes him choose.
Wren! Over here — or, I mean, wherever, no pressure—

lines.action: glances over, doesn't wave, but doesn't look away either
doesn't look up from their notebook, but there's a spot open across from them

lines.text: First time all year this feels like it's actually mine to decide.

options.text: I mean... I guess I could say something to Alvarez?
Partners are partners, Maya. That's just how it is.

a.text: Yeah, no, that's — that's very reasonable of you.
...Was that reasonable. That felt like something.

b.text: ...Okay. Sure. Whatever, it's not a big deal.
No for real, it's fine, it's — it's totally fine.

options.text: Okay. The real one? It's... a lot. But yeah.
Oh, you know. Cities, boxes, the usual. Anyway — should we start with the map idea?

a.text: No going back now, I guess.

b.text: That was easy. Too easy. Why does easy feel bad.

options.text: Actually — it's a good project. You should hear it before you laugh.
Yeah, no, it's — kind of a weird pick, I guess.

b.text: That laugh didn't feel like mine.

options.text: So here's my highlight reel — city number six, everybody, hold your applause.

a.text: No system for this part. Guess I'm making one up as I go.

b.emotion: eyes lingering a beat too long

options.text: Actually — can I say something? To Maya, I mean.
Yeah. Let's just... let it be normal again.

b.text: Buried. Not fixed. There's a difference, apparently.

options.text: I'll sit with Maya. Same as always.
New chair. New spot. Let's see who else wants in.

a.action: shifts the notebook over, making room

a.text: There it is. Barely have to say anything and there's already room.

b.text: Okay, FINALLY. Sit, sit — Jordan was about to have a nervous breakdown over here.

c.text: Wait, that's — okay, yeah, I'm doing that too actually.

c.emotion: wary glance, but doesn't say no

lines.text: Six schools. Five goodbyes. This is the first table I've sat at where nobody needed the bit first.
...I added a country. Named it after your suitcase thing. Don't make it weird.
lines.action: sliding the notebook across, casual

lines.text: Too late. Already weird. Tell me everything.
...Okay. So the river floods again, but this time—
lines.action: taps four times on the table, easier now, almost a rhythm instead of a brace

lines.text: Maya laughs at my joke before I even finish it. Same table. Same seat. Exactly where I aimed to land, months ago.
Okay, THAT'S the bit, right there — tell it again, tell it to Priya, she has to hear this.
lines.action: snaps fingers, delighted

lines.text: So — city number six, everybody, hold your applause—

lines.action: passes by with a tray, doesn't stop, doesn't look over, just keeps walking

lines.text: Still funny. Still easy. Nobody at this table knows what a Kettering River is. Guess that's the price tag. Didn't used to notice that. Now I do.
Didn't fix everything with Maya. Didn't need to, actually. Just needed it to be true.
Okay wait, is this a whole new table now? Are we a table? I want us to be a table.
lines.emotion: delighted, oblivious to the weight of it

lines.text: ...Don't push it, Jordan.
lines.action: arms crossed, but not performing

lines.text: ...This is weird. Good weird. But weird.
Everything's weird. Keep up.
Maybe belonging isn't a table you get picked for. Maybe it's just the chair you decide to pull up.

voiceNotes: Wren's INTERNAL lines carry the real emotional throughline — she narrates herself the way she can't narrate to anyone else, in short, dry, self-aware fragments ('Cool, cool, cool'). Her spoken dialogue stays quick and adaptive until choice-2/4/5, where the sentences get shorter and less polished — a tell that she's dropped the system. Alex's guardedness is built entirely on withheld words and the four-tap tic; every real disclosure from them should be preceded by a visible cost (setting down a pencil, a pause, 'don't make it weird'). Maya's dialogue should always sound performed until scene-11a, where her sentences get shorter and less punctuated with 'right?' — the drop in performance IS the vulnerability, no need to state it. Jordan should almost always be the one to break silence, and his jokes should be allowed to not quite land — that's the point of him. Mr. Alvarez's warmth should never fully resolve Wren's problems for her; he redirects, he doesn't rescue, which is what makes 'Here's the thing' land as strategic rather than paternal.

alternatives: I make friends fast because if I don't, I make friends never.
I get funny fast so nobody clocks how much I'm paying attention.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly less on-the-nose version if the emotional beat needs to land softer for pacing; keep original if the scene wants the full unguarded confession.

alternatives: That's kind of awful, actually. In a really impressive way.
...That's a lot to carry in a suitcase.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Alex's deadpan needs to register as genuine, not glib — alternates offered in case 'incredible... terrible' reads as too polished for a guarded character's first real reaction.

alternatives: If nobody's laughing, nobody's looking. That's just — that's the deal I made a long time ago.
I don't actually know how to be at a table where I'm not the one running it.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Offers a version that ties Maya's fear more explicitly to control/status if the scene needs a sharper contrast with Wren's own 'system,' underlining thematic parallel.
